transcript.clean_text: Welcome back to GCN Racing. After a day of rest, it’s time to resume with stage 10 of La Vuelta. On Sunday, we had another change in the race lead. Tadej Pogacar took an incredible solo stage victory over the mountains in Andorra, at just 20 years of age. Behind him, the best of the rest was Nairo Quintana, and he’d separated himself far enough from his rivals to take the race lead. However, both Lopez and Roglic had crashed, off camera, on a gravel sector, which had a large bearing on the stage and the new GC. Today, it was the race of truth. An individual time trial of 36.2kms in France, finishing in Pau. It was a rolling parcours, and also, apparently, the longest individual time trial of any worldtour race this year, so there was the chance of some very significant time gaps amongst the key riders. The early pace was set by the Frenchman Remi Cavagna of Deceuninck Quickstep. The 24 year old finished 5th at his national time trial championships this year, but today he was flying around the course, catching many of the riders who’d set out in front of him. By the time he got to the 2nd time check, he was over a minute clear of the next best rider, and by the finish, it was an even larger, gap, 1m39 ahead of Groupama FDJ’s Benjamin Thomas. Another man particularly motivated today was the New Zealand national champion in this discipline, Paddy Bevin of the CCC squad. He too was passing riders left right and centre, and Cavagna was sitting rather nervously in the hot seat at the finish line. Because it was close, very close, Bevin was 1 second behind at the first time check, 2 seconds in front at the 2nd. And, in the end, they both rode the final part of the course at exactly the same speed, Bevin coming across the line with a 2 second advantage over the Frenchman, outing him from the hot seat. And after that, there weren’t many riders setting off that would be capable of going any better. Except this man. Pimroz Roglic is the real deal when it comes to time trialling, but his primary aim would be to take time out of his rivals in the GC. He started the day in 2nd place overall, 6 seconds behind the red jersey, Nairo Quintana. And it was not a stage that Quintana was relishing - he’s not noted for his ability against the clock, and this, for him, would be all about limiting his losses as best he could. Which was going to be a tough task given the way that Roglic was riding in front of him. He was already 19 seconds up on the next best rider at the first intermediate time check after 12kms. Miguel Angel Lopez is also not a specialist in time trials. He’d headed out on the course 2 minutes in front of Roglic, but at this point, with 16kms to go, his time was being compared to Quintana, and he was 16 seconds to the good. Valverde was on a decent ride, but not out of this world. He would be almost a minute down by the 2nd check. Here’s Lopez, riding on that Argon 18 with the disc brakes. Tadej Pogocar is a brilliant climber, as we all saw on Sunday, but he’s also pretty bloomin’ good on the time trial bike too. He went through the first time check in 1st place, only later beaten by Roglic, but he did fade ever slightly. He’d lose over a minute to Bevin in the 2nd part of the race, crossing the line with a provisional 10th position, 1 minute and 4 seconds down. He’d do better than Valverde, though, who came through next, his time would be 1m13 behind Bevin’s, good enough for a provisional 12th place on the stage. The next man to come towards the line was Miguel Angel Lopez of Astana, or at least he was, until he was caught, and passed, by Roglic. We all knew that Roglic was going to take time out of his rivals today, but I don’t think we could have predicted by how much. He take a whopping 25 seconds out of Bevin by the line, and a lot more on the GC men. 2 minute exactly on Lopez, in fact, which meant the only man left out on the road was Quintana. And it wasn’t looking good, in fact it was a bit of a disaster. When he finally crossed the line, he’d lost over 3 minutes on Roglic, taking 27th position on the day. Don’t worry, though, this should set up some huge attacks in the moiuntains. Smiles all round for Roglic and his family, then, a job well done for him today. And so we have yet another leader of the Vuelta. Primoz Roglic in red and by quite some margin. That was the 6th new leader of the race in the space of 6 stages, apparently the first time that has ever happened at a Grand Tour. That, according to the very well respected cycling statistician and all round geek, Cillian Kelly. It was quite the day’s work for Roglic, who not only took the red jersey, but also the green and Tomorrow, it’s a hilly but not mountainous 180kms which starts from Saint Palais in France, before crossing the border and finishing in Urdax back in Spain. There are three categorised climbs, and for me, this one has breakaway written all over it. And therefore, my prediction is Thomas De Gendt. Safe bet, I reckon. See you tomorrow.
